item.title: “And, monks, when that monk dwells in these five things, four more things should be developed. The perception of foulness should be developed to abandon…
item.description:
“And, monks, when that monk dwells in these five things, four more things should be developed. The perception of foulness should be developed to abandon greed; loving-kindness should be developed to abandon ill-will; mindfulness of breathing should be developed to cut off thoughts; the perception of impermanence should be developed to uproot the conceit ‘I am.’ For a monk with the perception of impermanence, monks, the perception of not-self becomes established. For one with the perception of not-self, the conceit ‘I am’ is uprooted, and Nibbāna is attained in this very life.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 9.1
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Stop, thoughts of sensual desire! Stop, thoughts of ill-will! Stop, thoughts of harmfulness! Enough, thoughts of sensual desire! Enough, thoughts of ill-will! Enough, thoughts of…
item.description:
Stop, thoughts of sensual desire!
Stop, thoughts of ill-will!
Stop, thoughts of harmfulness!
Enough, thoughts of sensual desire!
Enough, thoughts of ill-will!
Enough, thoughts of harmfulness!

From: Dīgha Nikāya 17 – Mahāsudassanasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Suppose, monks, a fisherman throws a baited hook into a deep lake. A fish, seeing the bait, swallows the hook. Thus, monks, that fish, having…
item.description:
“Suppose, monks, a fisherman throws a baited hook into a deep lake. A fish, seeing the bait, swallows the hook. Thus, monks, that fish, having swallowed the fisherman’s hook, has fallen into misfortune, into calamity, subject to the fisherman’s will. So too, monks, there are these six hooks in the world, leading to the misfortune of beings, to the calamity of living beings. What six? Monks, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. If a monk delights in those forms, praises them, and dwells with lust for them — then that monk is called one who has swallowed Māra’s hook, has fallen into misfortune, into calamity, subject to the Evil One’s will. There are sounds … odors … tastes … touches … phenomena cognizable by the mind that are desirable … enticing. If a monk delights in those phenomena, praises them, and dwells with lust for them — then that monk is called one who has swallowed Māra’s hook, has fallen into misfortune, into calamity, subject to the Evil One’s will.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.230
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: - What gives birth to a person? What always runs about? What undergoes rebirth? What does a person fear? - Craving gives birth to a…
item.description:
- What gives birth to a person?
What always runs about?
What undergoes rebirth?
What does a person fear?
- Craving gives birth to a person,
The mind itself always runs about,
Beings undergo rebirth,
Suffering is what a person fears.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 1.55
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, what are the defilements that should be abandoned by restraint? Here, a monk, reflecting wisely, lives with the restraint of the eye faculty. Monks,…
item.description:
“Monks, what are the defilements that should be abandoned by restraint? Here, a monk, reflecting wisely, lives with the restraint of the eye faculty. Monks, if he lives without restraining the eye faculty, harmful, feverish defilements might arise. If he restrains the eye faculty, those harmful, feverish defilements are no more. These, monks, are called the defilements that should be abandoned by restraint.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 2 – Sabbāsavasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Then the Blessed One, knowing that it was Māra the Evil One, recited this verse to Māra: Whatever forms there are, In this world or…
item.description:
Then the Blessed One, knowing that it was Māra the Evil One, recited this verse to Māra:

Whatever forms there are,
In this world or the next,
With excellent colors,
Shining brightly in the sky —
All those forms,
Praised by Māra,
Are just bait cast out
To destroy the fish.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 2.30
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, when a monk has abandoned ignorance and aroused true knowledge, then with the abandonment of ignorance and the arising of true knowledge, he does…
item.description:
“Monks, when a monk has abandoned ignorance and aroused true knowledge, then with the abandonment of ignorance and the arising of true knowledge, he does not form any volitional formation, does not generate any kamma. Without forming, without generating, he does not cling to anything in the world. Without clinging, he is not agitated. Without agitation, he personally attains Nibbāna. He knows: ‘Birth is destroyed, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of being.’”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 12.51
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “‘Ignorance, ignorance,’ venerable sir, is said. What, venerable sir, is ignorance? And to what extent is one called ignorant?” - “Here, monk, an untaught ordinary…
item.description:
“‘Ignorance, ignorance,’ venerable sir, is said. What, venerable sir, is ignorance? And to what extent is one called ignorant?” - “Here, monk, an untaught ordinary person does not know as it actually is: ‘Form is subject to origination’ as form is subject to origination; he does not know as it actually is: ‘Form is subject to cessation’ as form is subject to cessation; he does not know as it actually is: ‘Form is subject to origination and cessation’ as form is subject to origination and cessation. This, monk, is called ignorance. And to that extent one is called ignorant.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.126
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, there are these two kinds of pleasure. What two? Sensual pleasure and the pleasure of renunciation. These, monks, are the two pleasures. The best…
item.description:
“Monks, there are these two kinds of pleasure. What two? Sensual pleasure and the pleasure of renunciation. These, monks, are the two pleasures. The best of these two pleasures, monks, is the pleasure of renunciation.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 2.65
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “And what, monks, are the defilements of the mind? Covetousness, unrighteous greed is a defilement of the mind; ill-will is a defilement of the mind;…
item.description:
“And what, monks, are the defilements of the mind? Covetousness, unrighteous greed is a defilement of the mind; ill-will is a defilement of the mind; anger is a defilement of the mind; hostility is a defilement of the mind; hypocrisy, spite, envy, stinginess, deceit, fraud, obstinacy, impetuosity, conceit, arrogance, vanity, heedlessness are defilements of the mind.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 7 – Vatthūpamasutta
📖 Read this sutta
